questions.question: The domain of the function $\sqrt {\cos x - } 1$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function {tex}\sqrt {\cos x - } 1{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
This function exists only if
This function exists only if
{tex}\cos x-1 \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos x \geq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos x>1{/tex}

OR

{tex}\cos x = 1{/tex} 
since maximum value of cosine function is 1 so, cos x >1 is not possible
so, 
 {tex}\cos x = 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x=2 n \pi(n \in I){/tex}

{tex}\forall {/tex} {tex}x \in R{/tex}  it is true 
So domain of f(x) is R.

questions.question: The range of the function f(x) = $\frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^2+x-6}$ is
questions.question_raw: The range of the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^2+x-6}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Let, y = {tex}\frac{x^{2}-3 x+2}{x^{2}+x-6}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x2 + x - 6) = x2 - 3x + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y - 1)x2 + (y + 3)x - 6y - 2 = 0
x = {tex}\frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4 a c}}{2 a}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{-(y+3) \pm \sqrt{(y+3)^{2}-4(y-1)(-6 y-2)}}{2(y-1)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{-(y+3) \pm \sqrt{25 y^{2}-10 y+1}}{2(y-1)}{/tex}
Now for x to be defined, y - 1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
Also,  
25y2 - 10y + 1 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (5y - 1)2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
But domain of f(x): x {tex}\ne{/tex} 2, -3
But, 
When, y = {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}, x = 2
So, range = R - {1, {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}}

questions.question: If f(x) = log$\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$, then $f\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = log{tex}\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right){/tex}, then {tex}f\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
f(x) = {tex}\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right){/tex}
{tex}f\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)=\log \left(\frac{1+\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}}{1-\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\log \left(\frac{1+x^2+2 x}{1+x^2-2 x}\right)=\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)^2{/tex}
{tex}=2 \log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)=2 f(x){/tex}

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{|x|}{x} ; x \neq 0$ then f(x) - f(-x) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac{|x|}{x} ; x \neq 0{/tex} then f(x) - f(-x) is equal to
questions.explanation:
f(x) = {tex}\frac{|x|}{x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{x}{/tex} = 1 
f(-x) = {tex}\frac{|-x|}{-x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{-x}{/tex} = -1
f(x) - f(-x) = 1 - (-1) = 1 + 1 = 2

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{x-1}{x+1}$ then $\left(f \frac{1}{f(x)}\right)$ equals
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac{x-1}{x+1}{/tex} then {tex}\left(f \frac{1}{f(x)}\right){/tex} equals
questions.explanation:
We have {tex}{f(x) = \frac{{x - 1}}{{x + 1}}}{/tex} then
{tex}f\left( {\frac{1}{{f(x)}}} \right){/tex}  = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{f(x)}-1}{\frac{1}{f(x)}+1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1-f(x)}{1+f(x)}{/tex} 
={tex}\frac{{1 - \frac{{x - 1}}{{x + 1}}}}{{1 + \frac{{x - 1}}{{x + 1}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x+1-x+1}{x+1+x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{2 x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}

questions.question: If $A = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + {y^2} = 25\} $ and $B = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = 144\} ,$ then $A \cap B$ contains
questions.question_raw: If {tex}A = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + {y^2} = 25\} {/tex} and {tex}B = \{ (x,y):{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = 144\} ,{/tex} then {tex}A \cap B{/tex} contains
questions.explanation:
We will solve equations in A and B simultaneously and find values of x and y. The no. of possible ordered pairs from these values will be elements in {tex}{A \cap B.}{/tex}

Now, From B,{tex}{{x^2} + 9{y^2} + {y^2} = \;144}{/tex} and 

From {tex}{A,\;{x^2} + {y^2}\; = \;25}{/tex}

{tex}{\therefore 9{y^2} + 25\; = \;144\; \Rightarrow 9{y^2}\; = 119}{/tex}

{tex}{ \Rightarrow y\; = \; \pm \sqrt {\frac{{119}}{9}} }{/tex}

{tex}{\therefore {x^2} + {y^2} = 25\; \Rightarrow {x^2}\; = 25 = \frac{{119}}{9}\; = \;\frac{{106}}{9}}{/tex}

{tex}{ \Rightarrow x\; = \; \pm \sqrt {\frac{{106}}{9}} }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} x has two value, y has two values

{tex}\therefore{/tex} possible ordered pairs = 4

{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}{A \cap B}{/tex} has 4 elements

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{x}{x-1}=\frac{1}{y}$, then f(y) =
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{x-1}=\frac{1}{y}{/tex}, then f(y) =
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{{x - 1}} = \frac{1}{y}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = {tex} \frac{{x - 1}}{x}{/tex} 
Now, f(x) = {tex} \frac{x}{{x - 1}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(y) = {tex}\frac{y}{y-1}=\frac{\frac{x-1}{z}}{\frac{x-1}{x}-1}=\frac{x-1}{x-1-x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-1}{-1}{/tex} = 1 - x

questions.question: The function f(x) = log$\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$ satisfies the equation
questions.question_raw: The function f(x) = log{tex}\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right){/tex} satisfies the equation
questions.explanation:
{tex}{f({x_1})\; + f({x_2}) = \;\log \left( {\frac{{1 + {x_1}}}{{1 - {x_1}}}} \right)\; + \log \left( {\frac{{1 + {x_2}}}{{1 - {x_2}}}} \right)}{/tex} 
{tex} = \;\log (1 + {x_1}) - \log (1 - {x_1}) {/tex}{tex}+ \log (1 + {x_2}) - \log (1 - {x_2}){/tex} 
{tex}{f\left( {\frac{{{x_1} + {x_2}}}{{1 + {x_1}{x_2}}}} \right) = \log \left( {\frac{{1 + \frac{{{x_1} + {x_2}}}{{1 + {x_1}{x_2}}}}}{{1 - \frac{{{x_1} + {x_2}}}{{1 + {x_1}{x_2}}}}}} \right) = \log \left[ {\frac{{1 + {x_1}{x_2} + {x_1} + {x_2}}}{{1 + {x_1}{x_2} - {x_1} - {x_2}}}} \right]}{/tex} 
{tex}{ = \;\log \left[ {\frac{{(1 + {x_1})(1 + {x_2})}}{{(1 - {x_1})(1 - {x_2})}}} \right]}{/tex} 
{tex}{ = \;\log (1 + {x_1})(1 + {x_2}) - \log (1 - {x_1})(1 - {x_2})}{/tex} 
{tex} = \;\log ((1 + {x_1}) + \log (1 + {x_2}) {/tex}{tex}- \log (1 - {x_1}) - \log (1 - {x_2}){/tex} 
{tex}{\therefore f({x_1})+ f({x_2}) = f\left( {\frac{{{x_1} + {x_2}}}{{1 + {x_1}{x_2}}}} \right)}{/tex}

questions.question: The domain of the function $\frac{1}{[x]}+\sqrt{2 x-x^2}$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function {tex}\frac{1}{[x]}+\sqrt{2 x-x^2}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) = {tex} \frac{1}{{\left[ x \right]}} + \sqrt {2x - {x^2}}{/tex} 
For f(x) to be real, x {tex}\in{/tex} [0, 1] and 2x - x2 = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\notin{/tex} [0, 1] and x2 - 2x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\notin{/tex} [0, 1] and x(x - 2) = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\notin{/tex} [0, 1] and x = 0, 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain = [1, 2]

questions.question: If A = {(x, y) : x^2 + y^2 = 5} and B = {(x, y) : 2x = 5y}, then A $\cap$ B contains
questions.question_raw: If A = {(x, y) : x2 + y2 = 5} and B = {(x, y) : 2x = 5y}, then A {tex}\cap{/tex} B contains
questions.explanation:
From A, x2 + y2 = 5 and from B, 2x = 5y
Now, 2x = 5y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{5}{2 y}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x2 + y2 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{5}{2 y}\right)^{2}{/tex} + y2 = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 29y2 = 20 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 29y2 = 20 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{5}{2}(\pm \sqrt{\frac{20}{29}}){/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Possible ordered pairs = four
But two ordered pair in which c is positive and y is negative will be rejected as it will not be satisifed by the equation in B. Therefore,
A {tex} \cap{/tex} B contains 2 elements.

questions.question: If f : R $ \to $ R is given by f(x) = |x| and A = {x $\in$ R : x < 0} , then f^-1(A) equals
questions.question_raw: If f : R {tex} \to {/tex} R is given by f(x) = |x| and A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x < 0} , then f-1(A) equals
questions.explanation:
Here,  A = {x {tex}\in{/tex} R : x < 0} {tex}\subseteq{/tex} co-domain
f-1(A) Contains those elements in R(domain) whose image is negative.
Since, f(x) = |x|
{tex}\therefore{/tex} no image of any elements of R(domain) is negative
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f-1(A) = {tex}\phi{/tex}

questions.question: If A = [a, b], B = [c, d], C = [d, e] then {(a, c), (a, d), (a,e), (b,c), (b, d), (b, e)} is equal to
questions.question_raw: If A = [a, b], B = [c, d], C = [d, e] then {(a, c), (a, d), (a,e), (b,c), (b, d), (b, e)} is equal to
questions.explanation:
A {tex}\times{/tex} (B {tex}\cup{/tex} C) = (A {tex}\times{/tex} B) {tex}\cup{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} C
= {a, b} {tex}\times{/tex} {c, d} {tex}\cup{/tex} {a, b} {tex}\times{/tex} {d, c}
= {(a, c), (a, d), (b, c), (b, d)} {tex}\cup{/tex} {(a, d), (a, c), (b, d), (b, c)}
= {(a, c), (a, d), (a, c), (b, c), (b, d), (b, e)}

questions.question: The function f(x) = ${10^x}$ from R to [0, $\infty $) is
questions.question_raw: The function f(x) = {tex}{10^x}{/tex} from R to [0, {tex}\infty {/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
We have, {tex}{f:R \to \left[ {0,\omega } \right]}{/tex} defined by,

{tex}{f(x) = \;{{10}^x}}{/tex}

one - one: {tex}{f({x_1})\; = \;f({x_2})}{/tex}

{tex}{ \Rightarrow \;{{10}^{{x_1}}}\; = {{10}^{{x_2}}}\; \Rightarrow \;{x_1}\; = \;{x_2}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is one - one

onto: Here, {tex}{0 \in \left[ {0,\omega } \right]}{/tex} (Co - domain)

Now, {tex}{f(x)\; = \;0\; \Rightarrow \;{{10}^x}\; = \;0}{/tex}

But this is not possible for {tex}{x \in R}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0 does not has pre - image

{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is not onto

questions.question: For all x $ \in $ (0, 1)
questions.question_raw: For all x {tex} \in {/tex} (0, 1)
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = x - log(1 + x) in [0, x]; x {tex} \in{/tex} (0, 1) clearly, f is continuous on [0, x] and differentiable on (0, x).
Therefore by Lagrange's mean value theorem, there exists c {tex} \in{/tex} (0, x) such that,
f'(c) = {tex} \;\frac{{{\text{f}}({\text{x}})\; - \;{\text{f}}(0)}}{{{\text{x}} - 0}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-\log (1+x)-0}{x}{/tex} {{tex}\because{/tex} f'(x) = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{1+x}{/tex}}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-\log (1+x)}{x}{/tex} = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{1+c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-\log (1+x)}{x}{/tex} > 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} c {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{1+c}{/tex} > 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - log (1 + x) > 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, 1)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (1 + x) < x

questions.question: The domain of the function $f(x) = \sqrt {x - 1} + \sqrt {6 - x} $
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function {tex}f(x) = \sqrt {x - 1} + \sqrt {6 - x} {/tex}
questions.explanation:
For f(x) to be real, we must have,
{tex}{x - 1 \geqslant 0\;and\;6 - x \geqslant 0}{/tex} 
{tex}{ \Rightarrow \;x \geqslant \varphi \;and\;x - 6\; \leqslant 6}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain = [1, 6]

questions.question: The minimum value of (x - $\alpha $) (x – $\beta $) is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of (x - {tex}\alpha {/tex}) (x – {tex}\beta {/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
Let f(x) = (x - {tex}\alpha{/tex})(x - {tex}\beta{/tex}) then, 
f'(x) = {tex}(x - \alpha ).1\; + 1.\;(x\; - \beta ){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 2x - ({tex}\alpha{/tex} + {tex}\beta{/tex}) 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f''(x) = 2
Now, f''(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - {tex}(\alpha+\beta){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}{/tex} 
At x = {tex}\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}{/tex}, f''(x) = 2 > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex} \frac{{\alpha \; + \beta }}{2}{/tex} is point of minimum value and minimum value is,
{tex}f\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-\alpha\right)\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}-\beta\right){/tex} 
= {tex}\left(\frac{\beta-\alpha}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{4}(\alpha-\beta)^{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If f (x) = ${(25 - {x^4})^{1/4}}$ for 0 < x <$\sqrt 5 $, then $f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)$ =
questions.question_raw: If f (x) = {tex}{(25 - {x^4})^{1/4}}{/tex} for 0 < x <{tex}\sqrt 5 {/tex}, then {tex}f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right){/tex} =
questions.explanation:
fof(x) = f(f(x)) = {tex}\left\{ {f{{\left( {25 - {x^4}} \right)}^{\frac{1}{4}}}} \right\}{/tex}
= {tex}\left\{25-\left(25-x^{4}\right)\right\}^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(x^{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} = x 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}f\left(f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= 2-1

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac {x} {x - 1}$ then $\frac{f(a)}{f(a+1)}$ =
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac {x} {x - 1}{/tex} then {tex}\frac{f(a)}{f(a+1)}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{f(a)}{f(a+1)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\frac{a}{{a - 1}}}}{{\frac{{a + 1}}{{a + 1 - 1}}}} = \frac{{\frac{a}{{a - 1}}}}{{\frac{{a + 1}}{a}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{a}{a-1} \times \frac{a}{a+1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{a^{2}-1}{/tex} 
f(a2) = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{a^{2}-1}{/tex}

questions.question: The minimum value of sin x + cos x is
questions.question_raw: The minimum value of sin x + cos x is
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}{f(x)\; = \;\sin \;x + \;cos\;x}{/tex}

{tex}{\therefore \;f'(x)\; = {\text{co}}s\;x\; - \;\sin \;x}{/tex}

{tex}{ \Rightarrow \;f''\;(x)\; = \; - \sin \;x\; - \;\cos \;x}{/tex}

Now, {tex}{f'(x)\; = 0}{/tex}

{tex}{ \Rightarrow \;\cos \;x\; - \;\sin \;x\; = \;0\; \Rightarrow \;\sin \;x\; = \;\cos \;x\; \Rightarrow \;\tan \;x\; = \;1}{/tex}

{tex}{ \Rightarrow \;x\; = n\pi \; + \;\frac{\pi }{4},\;{\text{n}} \in \;{\text{z}}}{/tex}

At {tex}{{\text{x}}\; = \pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4},}{/tex}

{tex}{{\text{f''}}\;({\text{x}})\; = \; - \sin \;\left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; - \;\cos \;\;\left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}{/tex}

{tex}{ = \;\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; + \cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; + \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \sqrt {2\;} \;\; > \;0}{/tex}

{tex}{\therefore \;{\text{x}}\; = \;\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}}{/tex} is point of minimum

Minimum value {tex}{ = \;\sin \left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\;\; + \cos \left( {\pi \; + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}{/tex}

{tex}{ = \; - \sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\; - \;\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{4}} \right)}{/tex}

{tex}{ = \; - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \; - \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }}\; = \; - \sqrt 2 }{/tex}

one - one : Let n1, n2 {tex}\in{/tex} N

Case II: n1 is odd, n2 is odd

But f(n1) takes values, -1, -2, -3,..... f(n2) take values, 0, 1, 2, 3,.....
{tex}\therefore {n_1} \ne {n_2} \Rightarrow f({n_1}) \ne f({n_2}){/tex} 
Similarly n, is odd, n2, is even, then {tex}\therefore {n_1} \ne {n_2} \Rightarrow f({n_1}) \ne f({n_2}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f is one - one
onto: f(n) =  {{tex}\frac{n-1}{2}{/tex}, when n is odd; {tex}\frac{-n}{2}{/tex}, when n is even} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(1) = 0, f(2) = 1, f(5) = 2, f(7) = 3, f(9) - 4, ... f(2)= -1, f(4) = -2, f(6) = -3, f(8) = -4, ......
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Range of f = {......, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ....) = z

questions.question: If f : $R \to R$ satisfies f (x + y) = f (x ) + f (y) for all x, y $ \in $ R and f (1) = 7, then $\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {f(r)} $ is

Given f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y).....(i) and f(1) = 7
Put x=1, y=1 in equation (i), we obtain quad
f(1+1) = f(1) + f(1) = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(2) = 14
Similarly f(1 + 1 + 1) = f(2) + f(1) = 14 + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(3) = 21
Since we have f(1) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 7, f(2) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 14, f(3) = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 21,........
we can get f(n) = n {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 7n
Now {tex}\sum \limits_{r=1}^{n}{/tex} f(r) = f(1) + f(2) + f(3) + ..... + f(n)
7r=1 + 14 + 21 + ..... + 7n
= 7[1 + 2 + 3 + ..... + n] = 7 {tex}\frac{n(n+1)}{2}{/tex}

Surjectivity: Let y{tex}\; \in {/tex} R, Then {tex}f(x) = y \Rightarrow 3x = y \Rightarrow x = \frac{y}{3}{/tex}, Clearly, {tex}\frac{y}{3} \in R\;for\;any\;y \in R{/tex}  such that {tex}f\;\left( {\frac{y}{3}} \right) = 3\left( {\frac{y}{3}} \right) = y\;.{/tex} So, Let f : {tex}{\text{ }}R{\text{ }} \to {\text{ }}R{/tex} is onto.

questions.question: Let f(x) = a^x(a > 0) be written as f(x) = f_1(x) + f_2(x), where f_1(x) is an even function and f_2(x) is an odd function. Then f_1(x + y) + f_1(x - y) equals
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = ax(a > 0) be written as f(x) = f1(x) + f2(x), where f1(x) is an even function and f2(x) is an odd function. Then f1(x + y) + f1(x - y) equals
questions.explanation:
Given, function f(x) = ax, a > 0 is written as sum of an even and odd functions f1(x) and f2(x) respectively.
Clearly, f1(x) = {tex}\frac{a^{x}+a^{-x}}{2}{/tex} and f2(x) = {tex}\frac{a^{x}-a^{-x}}{2}{/tex}
So, f1(x + y) + f1(x - y)
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[a^{x+y}+a^{-(x+y)}\right]+\frac{1}{2}\left[a^{x-y}+a^{-(x-y)}\right] {/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[a^{x} a^{y}+\frac{1}{a^{x} a^{y}}+\frac{a^{x}}{a^{y}}+\frac{a^{y}}{a^{x}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[a^{x}\left(a^{y}+\frac{1}{a^{y}}\right)+\frac{1}{a^{x}}\left(\frac{1}{a^{y}}+a^{y}\right)\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(a^{x}+\frac{1}{a^{x}}\right)\left(a^{y}+\frac{1}{a^{y}}\right){/tex}
= {tex}2\left(\frac{a^{x}+a^{-x}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{a^{y}+a^{-y}}{2}\right){/tex}
= 2f1(x).f1(y)

questions.question: Let f ($\theta$) = sin$\theta$ (sin$\theta$ + sin 3$\theta$). Then, f ($\theta$)

{tex}=(\sin 2 \theta)^{2} \geq 0{/tex}
Which is true for all {tex}\theta{/tex}.

questions.question: Let $\sum_{k=1}^{10} f(a+k)=16\left(2^{10}-1\right)$, where the function f satisfies f(x + y) = f(x) f(y ) for all natural numbers x, y and f(1) = 2. Then, the natural number ‘a’ is

questions.explanation:
Given, {tex}f(x+y)=f(x) \cdot f(y){/tex}
Let {tex}f(x)=\lambda^{x}{/tex} [where {tex}\lambda>0{/tex}]
{tex}\because \quad f(1)=2{/tex} (given)
{tex}\therefore \lambda=2{/tex}
So, {tex}\sum\limits_{k = 1}^{10} f (a + k) = \sum\limits_{k = 1}^{10} {{\lambda ^{a + k}}} = {\lambda ^a}\left( {\sum\limits_{k = 1}^{10} {{\lambda ^k}} } \right){/tex}
{tex}=2^{a}\left[2^{1}+2^{2}+2^{3}+\ldots \ldots+2^{10}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=2^{a}\left[\frac{2\left(2^{10}-1\right)}{2-1}\right]{/tex}
[by using formula of sum of n-terms of a GP having first term ‘a’ and common ratio V, is 
{tex}\left.S_{n}=\frac{a\left(r^{n}-1\right)}{r-1}, \text { where } r>1\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2^{a+1}\left(2^{10}-1\right)=16\left(2^{10}-1\right){/tex} (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2^{a+1}=16=2^{4} \Rightarrow a+1=4 \Rightarrow a=3{/tex}

questions.question: Let a,b,c $\in$ R. If f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c be such that a + b + c = 3 and f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) + xy, $\forall$ x, y$\in$R, then $\sum\limits_{n=1}^{10} f(n)$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: Let a,b,c {tex}\in{/tex} R. If f(x) = ax2 + bx + c be such that a + b + c = 3 and f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) + xy, {tex}\forall{/tex} x, y{tex}\in{/tex}R, then {tex}\sum\limits_{n=1}^{10} f(n){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have, fix) = ax2 + bx + c
Now, f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) + xy
Put y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = f(x) + f(0) + 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(0) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 0
Again, put y = -x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(0) = f(x)+ f(-x) - x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = ax2 + bx + ax2 - bx - x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ax2 - x2 = 0
{tex} \Rightarrow a = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Also, a + b + c = 3
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} + b + 0 = 3 \Rightarrow b = \frac{5}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x) = \frac{{{x^2} + 5x}}{2}{/tex}
Now, {tex}f(n) = \frac{{{n^2} + 5n}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}{n^2} + \frac{5}{2}n{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \sum\limits_{n = 1}^{10} {f(n)} = \frac{1}{2}\sum\limits_{n = 1}^{10} {{n^2} + \frac{5}{2}} \sum\limits_{n = 1}^{10} n {/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{{10 \times 11 \times 21}}{6} + \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{{10 \times 11}}{2}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{385}}{2} + \frac{{275}}{2} = \frac{{660}}{2} = 330{/tex}

questions.question: Let $f(x)=\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}$, x $\ne$ -1 Then, for what value of $\alpha$ is f[f(x)] = x?

Given, f (x) = {tex}\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}f[f(x)]=f\left(\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}\right)=\frac{\alpha\left(\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}\right)}{\frac{\alpha x}{x+1}+1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{\alpha^{2} x}{x+1}}{\frac{\alpha x+(x+1)}{x+1}}=\frac{\alpha^{2} x}{(\alpha+1) x+1} {/tex} = x (given) ...(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha^{2} x=(\alpha+1) x^{2}+x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x\left[\alpha^{2}-(\alpha+1) x-1\right]=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x(\alpha+1)(\alpha-1-x)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} - 1 = 0 and {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = -1 
as  {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1 does not satisfy the Eq. (i).

questions.question_raw: Let f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R be defined by f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}, x {tex}\in{/tex} R. Then, range of f is

Ilnd Method f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\frac{1}{x}}{/tex}

If x = 0, then f(x) = {tex}\frac{0}{1+0}{/tex} = 0 
Thus, {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \leq f(x) \leq \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Illrd Method

{tex}\because{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R, so D {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - 4y2 {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 - 2y) (1 + 2y) {tex}\geq{/tex} 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\in{/tex} {tex}\left[-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right]{/tex}

So, range is {tex}\left[-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right]{/tex}.

questions.question: Let f : R - {n} $\to$ R be a function defined by $f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}$, where m $\ne$ n. Then,
questions.question_raw: Let f : R - {n} {tex}\to{/tex} R be a function defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}{/tex}, where m {tex}\ne{/tex} n. Then,
questions.explanation:
Given that f : R - {n} {tex}\to{/tex} R where
{tex}f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}{/tex}, such that m {tex}\ne{/tex} n
Let f(x) = f(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-m}{x-n}=\frac{y-m}{y-n}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - m)(y - n) = (x - n)(y - m)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - xn - my + mn = xy - xm - ny + mn
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y
So, f is one-one.
{tex}f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{n}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x - n) = (x - m)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - ny = x - m
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y -1) = ny - m
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{ny}-\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{y}-1}, \mathrm{y} \neq 1{/tex}
For y = 1 , no x is defined.
So, f is into.

questions.question: Let f : Z $\to$ Z be given by $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{x}{2}, \text { if } x \text { is even } \\ 0, \text { if } x \text { is odd } \end{array}\right.$. Then f is

questions.question: f : R $ \rightarrow $ R : f (x) = x^2 is

f: R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R : f(x) = x2 
One-One function
Let p, q be two arbitrary elements in R
Then, f(p) = f(q)

Since v {tex}\in{/tex} R
If v = 2, {tex}\sqrt{v}{/tex} = 1.1414, which is not possible as x {tex}\in{/tex} R
Thus, f(x) is not onto function. It is into function.

questions.question: let A = R - (3) and B = R - 1 Then, f : A $\rightarrow B: f(x)=\frac{(x-2)}{(x-3)} \text { is }$
questions.question_raw: let A = R - (3) and B = R - 1 Then, f : A {tex}\rightarrow B: f(x)=\frac{(x-2)}{(x-3)} \text { is }{/tex}
questions.explanation:
Given: Let A = R - {3}, B = R - {1}
f: A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B f(x) =  {tex}\frac{x-2}{x-3}{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} A
Step 1: Injective or One-One function:
Let f(x) = f(y), x, y {tex}\ne{/tex} 3
{tex}\frac{x-2}{x-3}=\frac{y-2}{y-3}{/tex} 
(x - 2)(y - 3) = (y -  2)(x - 3)
xy - 2y - 3x + 6 = xy - 2x - 3y + 6xy - 2y - 3x + 6 = xy - 2x - 3y + 6
- 2x + 3y = - 2x + 3x - 2x + 3y = - 2x + 3x
x = y
Hence f is one-one 
Step 2 : Surjective or One-to function:
Let y {tex}\in{/tex} B such that y {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
y =  {tex}\frac{x-2}{x-3}{/tex} 
y(x - 3) = (x - 2)y(x - 3) = (x - 2)
yx - 3y = x - 2yx - 3y = x - 2
yx - x = 3y - 2yx - x = 3y - 2
x(y - 1) = 3y - 2
x = {tex}\frac{3 y-2}{y-1}{/tex} 
x {tex}\in{/tex} A since y {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
Hence there exists x {tex}\in{/tex} A for every y {tex}\in{/tex} B such that f(x) = y
f is onto
Hence f is bijective.

questions.question: Let A and B be two non-empty sets and let f : (A x B) $\rightarrow$ (B x A ) : f(a, b) = (b, a). Then, f is
questions.question_raw: Let A and B be two non-empty sets and let f : (A x B) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (B x A ) : f(a, b) = (b, a). Then, f is
questions.explanation:
Here f: A {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} A Such that
f(x, b) = (b, a)
One-One: - Let f(a1, b1) = f(a2, b2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b1, a1) = (b2, a2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a1 = a2 and b1 = b2  
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a1, b1) = (a2, b2) 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is one - one.
Onto: - Let (x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} A then
(x, y) = f(a, b)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x, y) = (b, a)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = b and y = a
So, for every (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} A
There exists (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is onto
Hence f is a Bijection.

questions.question: Let n (A) = m, and n (B) = n. Then the total number of non-empty relations that can be defined from A to B is
questions.question_raw: Let n (A) = m, and n (B) = n. Then the total number of non-empty relations that can be defined from A to B is
questions.explanation:
We have, n(A) = m and n(B) = n
n(A {tex}\times{/tex} B) = n(A) {tex}\cdot{/tex} n(B) = mn
Total number of relation from A to B = Number of subsets of A {tex}\times{/tex} B = 2mn
So, total number of non-empty relations = 2mn - 1

questions.question: If [x]^2 - 5[x] + 6 = 0, where [ . ] denote the greatest integer function, then
questions.question_raw: If [x]2 - 5[x] + 6 = 0, where [ . ] denote the greatest integer function, then
questions.explanation:
We have [x]2 - 5[x] + 6 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [(x - 3)(x - 2)]= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [x] = 2, 3
For [x] = 2, x {tex}\in{/tex} [2, 3)
For [x] = 3, x {tex}\in{/tex} [3, 4)
x {tex}\in{/tex} [2, 3){tex}\cup{/tex}[3, 4)
or x{tex}\in{/tex}[2, 4)

questions.question: Range of f(x) $=\frac{1}{1-2 \cos x}$ is
questions.question_raw: Range of f(x) {tex}=\frac{1}{1-2 \cos x}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We know that, {tex}-1 \leq \cos x \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -1 \leq-\cos x \leq 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -2 \leq-2 \cos x \leq 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -1 \leq 1-2 \cos x \leq 3{/tex}
Now f(x) {tex}=\frac{1}{1-2 \cos x}{/tex} is defined if
{tex}-1 \leq 1-2 \cos x<0{/tex} or {tex}0<1-2 \cos x \leq 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -1 \geq \frac{1}{1-2 \cos x}>-\infty{/tex} or {tex}\infty>\frac{1}{1-2 \cos x} \geq \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1-2 \cos x} \in(-\infty,-1] \cup\left[\frac{1}{3}, \infty\right){/tex}

questions.question:
Let f(x) $=\sqrt{1+x^{2}},$ then
[[Hint : find f (xy) = $\sqrt{1+x^{2} y^{2}}$, f(x).f(y) $=\sqrt{1+x^{2} y^{2}+x^{2}+y^{2}}]$
questions.question_raw:
Let f(x) {tex}=\sqrt{1+x^{2}},{/tex} then
[[Hint : find f (xy) = {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2} y^{2}}{/tex}, f(x).f(y) {tex}=\sqrt{1+x^{2} y^{2}+x^{2}+y^{2}}]{/tex}
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) {tex}=\sqrt{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}f(x y)=\sqrt{1+x^{2} y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}f(x) \cdot f(y)=\sqrt{1+x^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1+y^{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{\left(1+x^{2}\right)\left(1+y^{2}\right)}=\sqrt{1+x^{2}+y^{2}+x^{2} y^{2}}{/tex}
Now {tex}\sqrt{1+x^{2} y^{2}} \leq \sqrt{1+x^{2}+y^{2}+x^{2} y^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x y) \leq f(x) \cdot f(y){/tex}

questions.question: Domain of $\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}(a>0)$ is
questions.question_raw: Domain of {tex}\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}(a>0){/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have f(x) {tex}=\sqrt{a^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex}
Clearly f(x) is defined if {tex}a^{2}-x^{2} \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \leq a^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -a \leq x \leq a{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} a > 0]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f is [-a, a]

questions.question: If f (x) = ax + b, where a and b are integers, f (-1) = -5 and f (3) = 3, then a and b are equal to
questions.question_raw: If f (x) = ax + b, where a and b are integers, f (-1) = -5 and f (3) = 3, then a and b are equal to
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) = ax + b
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(-1) = a(-1) + b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -5 = -a + b ...(i)
Also, f(3) = a(3) + b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 = 3a + b ...(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
a = 2 and b = -3

questions.question: The domain of the function f defined by f(x) = $\sqrt{4-x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}$ is equal to
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function f defined by f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{4-x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x){tex}=\sqrt{4-x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex}
f(x) is defined if {tex}4-x \geq 0{/tex} and x2 - 1 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x-4 \leq 0{/tex} and (x + 1)(x - 1) > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \leq 4{/tex} and (x < -1 or x > 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f {tex}=(-\infty,-1) \cup(1,4]{/tex}

questions.question: The domain and range of real function f defined by f (x) $=\sqrt{x-1}$ is given by
questions.question_raw: The domain and range of real function f defined by f (x) {tex}=\sqrt{x-1}{/tex} is given by
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) {tex}=\sqrt{x-1}{/tex}
Clearly, f(x) is defined if {tex}x-1 \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x \geq 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f {tex}=[1, \infty){/tex}
Now for {tex}x \geq 1, x-1 \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{x-1} \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Range of f {tex}=[0, \infty){/tex}

questions.question: The domain of the function f given by f (x) = $\frac{x^{2}+2 x+1}{x^{2}-x-6}$
questions.question_raw: The domain of the function f given by f (x) = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+2 x+1}{x^{2}-x-6}{/tex}

f(x) is not defined, if x2 - x - 6 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 3)(x + 2) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = -2, 3

questions.question: The domain and range of the function f given by f(x) $=2-|x-5|$ is
questions.question_raw: The domain and range of the function f given by f(x) {tex}=2-|x-5|{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
We have, f(x) = {tex}2-|x-5|{/tex}
Clearly, f(x) is defined for all x{tex}\in{/tex}R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Domain of f = R
Now, {tex}|x-5| \geq 0, \forall x \in R{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow -|x-5| \leq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2-|x-5| \leq 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x) \leq 2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Range of f {tex}=(-\infty, 2]{/tex}

questions.question: The domain for which the functions defined by f(x) = 3x^2 - 1 and g (x) = 3 + x are equal is
questions.question_raw: The domain for which the functions defined by f(x) = 3x2 - 1 and g (x) = 3 + x are equal is

We have, f(x) = 3x{tex}^2{/tex} - 1 and g(x) = 3 + x
f(x) = g(x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - 1 = 3 + x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 - x - 4 = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (3x - 4)(x + 1) = 0
{tex}\therefore x=-1, \frac{4}{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If A = {1, 2, 4}, B = {2, 4, 5}, c = {2, 5}, then (A - B) $\times$ (B - C) is
questions.question_raw: If A = {1, 2, 4}, B = {2, 4, 5}, c = {2, 5}, then (A - B) {tex}\times{/tex} (B - C) is
questions.explanation:
(A – B) = {1, 2, 4} – {2, 4, 5} = {1}
and (B - C) = {2, 4, 5} – {2, 5}
= {4}
Now, (A−B) {tex}\times{/tex} (B − C) = {1} {tex}\times{/tex} {4}
= {(1, 4)}
Since, it is a set so it is written in curly braces.
Therefore, option B is correct.

questions.question: If R is a relation on the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9} given by x R y $\Leftrightarrow$ y = 3 x, then R =
questions.question_raw: If R is a relation on the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9} given by x R y {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y = 3 x, then R =

Here, y = 3x;
If x = 1; then y = 3.
If x = 2; then y = 6.
If x = 3; then y = 9.
Therefore the required relation will be R = {(1, 3), (2, 6), (3, 9)}.

questions.question: Let A = {1, 2, 3}, B = {1, 3, 5}. If relation R from A to B is given by R = {(1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 3)}. Then, R^-1 is
questions.question_raw: Let A = {1, 2, 3}, B = {1, 3, 5}. If relation R from A to B is given by R = {(1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 3)}. Then, R-1 is

Inverse of a relation is given by interchanging the element’s position in each pair.
Ex: Inverse of relation P = {(x, y)} is given by P−1 = {(y, x)}.
Therefore, R−1 = {(3, 1), (5, 2), (3, 3)}.

questions.question: If R = {(x, y): x, y $\in$ Z, x^2 + y^2 $\leq$ 4} is a relation on Z, then domain of R is
questions.question_raw: If R = {(x, y): x, y {tex}\in{/tex} Z, x2 + y2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 4} is a relation on Z, then domain of R is
questions.explanation:
Domain of R is a set constituting all values of x.
Here, possible values for x by equation x2 + y2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 4 will be 0, 1, -1, 2, -2.
So, Domain of R is : {-2, -1, 0, 1, 2}.

questions.question: A relation R is defined from {2, 3, 4, 5} to {3, 6, 7, 10} by: x R y $\Leftrightarrow$ x is relatively prime to y. Then domain of R is
questions.question_raw: A relation R is defined from {2, 3, 4, 5} to {3, 6, 7, 10} by: x R y {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} x is relatively prime to y. Then domain of R is
questions.explanation:
Relatively prime numbers are those numbers that have only 1 as the common factor.
So, according to this definition we get to know that (2, 3), (2, 7), (3, 7), (3, 10), (4, 3), (4, 7), (5, 3), (5, 6), (5, 7) are relatively prime.
So, R = {(2, 3), (2, 7), (3, 7), (3, 10), (4, 3), (4, 7), (5, 3), (5, 6) ,(5, 7)}.
Therefore, the Domain of R is the values of x or the first element of the ordered pair.
So, Domain = {2, 3, 4, 5}

questions.question: Let R be a relation on N defined by x + 2y = 8. The domain of R is
questions.question_raw: Let R be a relation on N defined by x + 2y = 8. The domain of R is
questions.explanation:
We have , x + 2y = 8
y = {tex}\frac{8-x}{2}{/tex}
since, x and y are Natural numbers, So x must be an even number.
if x = 2, y = 3;
if x = 4, y = 2;
if x = 6, y = 1.
So, relation R = {(2, 3), (4, 2), (6, 1)}
Hence, the domain of R is {2, 4, 6}.

questions.question: R is a relation from {11, 12, 13} to {8, 10, 12} defined by y = x - 3. Then, R^-1 is
questions.question_raw: R is a relation from {11, 12, 13} to {8, 10, 12} defined by y = x - 3. Then, R-1 is

Since, y = x – 3;
Therefore, for x = 11, y = 8.
For x = 12, y = 9.   [ But the value y = 9 does not exist in the given set.]
For x = 13, y =10.
So, we have R = {(11, 8), (13, 10)}
Now, R−1 = {(8, 11), (10, 13)}.

questions.question: If the set A has p elements, B has q elements, then the number of elements in A $\times$ B is
questions.question_raw: If the set A has p elements, B has q elements, then the number of elements in A {tex}\times{/tex} B is
questions.explanation:
Since A has p elements, so each element of A will make a pair with each element of set B, and so other elements of A will do the same.
This all forms q + q + q + q +... (p-times) ordered pairs.
Therefore, we will have a total of pq elements in A {tex}\times{/tex} B.

questions.question: If R is a relation from a finite set A having m elements to a finite set B having n elements, then the number of relations from A to B is
questions.question_raw: If R is a relation from a finite set A having m elements to a finite set B having n elements, then the number of relations from A to B is
questions.explanation:
Since we know that a relation from A to B consists of mn ordered pairs if they contain m and n elements respectively.
Each subset of those mn pairs will be a relation. so, each pair has two choices, either to be in that particular relation or not.
So, we have a total of 2mn relations.

= cos (log x2 ) cos ( logy2) - {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left\{\cos \left(\log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{y^{2}}\right)\right)+\cos \left(\log x^{2} y^{2}\right)\right\}{/tex}
= {tex}\cos (2 \log x) \cos (2 \log y)-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left\{\cos \left(\log x^{2}-\log y^{2}\right)+\cos \left(\log x^{2}+\log y^{2}\right)\right\}{/tex}

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{2^{x}+2^{-x}}{2}$ Then f(x + y) f (x - y) is equals to
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = {tex}\frac{2^{x}+2^{-x}}{2}{/tex} Then f(x + y) f (x - y) is equals to
questions.explanation:
{tex}f(x+y) f(x-y)=\left(\frac{2^{x+y}+2^{-(x+y)}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2^{x-y}+2^{-(x-y)}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{2^{x+y}+\frac{1}{2^{ x+y}}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{2^{x-y}+\frac{1}{2 ^{x-y}}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{2^{2(x+y)}+1}{2 \cdot 2^{(x+y)}}\right)\left(\frac{2^{2(x-y)}+1}{2 \cdot 2^{(x-y)}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{2^{2(x+y)} 2^{2(x-y)}+2^{2(x+y)}+2^{2(x-y)}+1}{4 \cdot 2^{(x+y)} 2^{(x-y)}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{2^{4 x}+2^{2(x+y)}+2^{2(x-y)}+1}{4 \cdot 2^{2 x}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{2^{2 x}+2^{2 y}+2^{-2 y}+2^{-2 x}}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2^{2 x}+2^{-2 x}}{2}+\frac{2^{2 y}+2^{-2 y}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\{f(2 x)+f(2 y)\}{/tex}

{tex}2 f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)-3 f(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}} {/tex} ... (2)
Multiply eqn 1 by 2 and eqn 2 by 3 and add them .

questions.question: Let f: R $\to$ R be defined as f(x) = 3^-|x| - 3^x + sgn (e^-x) + 2 (where sgn x denotes signum function of x). Then which one of the following is correct?
questions.question_raw: Let f: R {tex}\to{/tex} R be defined as f(x) = 3-|x| - 3x + sgn (e-x) + 2 (where sgn x denotes signum function of x). Then which one of the following is correct?

options.text: f is surjective but not injective
f i s neither injective nor surjective
f is injective as well as surjective
f is injective but not surjective

questions.explanation:
we have {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} {3,} & {x \leq 0} \\ {3^{-x}-3^{x}+3,} & {x>0} \end{array}\right.{/tex} (As sgn (e-x) = 1 {tex}\forall x \in R{/tex})
Clearly f(x) is many one.
For x > 0, f(x) is decreasing, hence range of f(x) for x > 0 is {tex}(-\infty, 3){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Range of the function f(x) is {tex}(-\infty, 3]{/tex}, which is subset of R.
Hence, f is neither injective nor surjective.

questions.question: If f (x) = $\pi\left(\frac{\sqrt{x+7}-4}{x-9}\right)$, then the range of function y = sin (2f(x)) is:
questions.question_raw: If f (x) = {tex}\pi\left(\frac{\sqrt{x+7}-4}{x-9}\right){/tex}, then the range of function y = sin (2f(x)) is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}f(x) = \pi \left[ {\frac{{\sqrt {x + 7} - 4}}{{x - 9}}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x + 7 \geqslant 0 \Rightarrow x \geqslant - 7{/tex}
and denominator {tex}x - 9 \ne 0 \Rightarrow x \ne 9{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \in \left[ { - 7,\infty } \right) - \left\{ 9 \right\}{/tex}
Now {tex}f( - 7) = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}
On increasing value of 'x', value of f(x) decreases.
i.e., for {tex}x \to \infty {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow f(x) \in \left[ {\frac{\pi }{4},0} \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2f(x) \in \left[ {\frac{\pi }{2},0} \right){/tex}
Hence {tex}\sin (2f(x)) \in \left( {0,1} \right]{/tex}

questions.question:
The sum of all possible values of n where n $\in$ N, x > 0 and 10 < n $\leq$ 100 such that the equation [2x^2] + x - n = 0 has a solution, is equal to:
[Note : [x] denotes largest integer less than or equal to x.]
questions.question_raw:
The sum of all possible values of n where n {tex}\in{/tex} N, x > 0 and 10 < n {tex}\leq{/tex} 100 such that the equation [2x2] + x - n = 0 has a solution, is equal to:
[Note : [x] denotes largest integer less than or equal to x.]
questions.explanation:
We have {tex}\left[2 x^{2}\right]+x-n=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x has to be an integer.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 2x2 + x = x (2x + 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n can be 21, 36, 55, 78 corresponding to x = 3, 4, 5, 6.
Hence, sum of all possible values of n is equal to 190.

questions.question: If the range of the function f (x) = $\frac{x-1}{p-x^{2}+1}$ does not contain any values belonging to the interval $\left[-1, \frac{-1}{3}\right]$ then the true set of values of p, is :
questions.question_raw: If the range of the function f (x) = {tex}\frac{x-1}{p-x^{2}+1}{/tex} does not contain any values belonging to the interval {tex}\left[-1, \frac{-1}{3}\right]{/tex} then the true set of values of p, is :
questions.explanation:
Let t = f(x)
{tex}t=\frac{x-1}{p-x^{2}+1} \Rightarrow t x^{2}+x-1-t(p+1)=0{/tex}
so {tex}1+4 t[1+(p+1) t] \geq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 4 p t^{2}+(2 t+1)^{2} \geq 0{/tex}
above inequality is true for t = 0
now when t {tex}\neq{/tex} 0, then substitute
{tex}\frac{1}{2 t}=-(y=1), y \neq\left[-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right]{/tex}
We have {tex}p+y^{2} \geq 0{/tex} (so p must be negative otherwise it is true for all y)
so
{tex}y \in(-\infty,-\sqrt{-p}] \cup[\sqrt{-p}, \infty){/tex}
so {tex}-\sqrt{-p}<-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt{-p}>\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
at last we have
{tex}-p>\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow p<-\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If the range of function f (x) = $\frac{x^{2}+x+c}{x^{2}+2 x+c}, x \in R$ is $\left[\frac{5}{6}, \frac{3}{2}\right]$, then c is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If the range of function f (x) = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+c}{x^{2}+2 x+c}, x \in R{/tex} is {tex}\left[\frac{5}{6}, \frac{3}{2}\right]{/tex}, then c is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}y = \frac{{{x^2} + x + c}}{{{x^2} + 2x + c}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x2y + 2xy + cy = x2 + x + c
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} x2(y - 1) + x(2y - 1) + c(y - 1) = 0
Now discriminant {tex}D \geqslant 0{/tex} as {tex}x \in R{/tex}
i.e., (2y - 1)2 - 4(y - 1)c(y - 1) {tex} \geqslant{/tex} 0
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 4y2 + 1 - 4y - 4c[y2 + 1 - 2y] {tex}\ge{/tex} 0
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 4y2 + 1 - 4y - 4cy2 - 4c + 8cy {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 .... (i)
Now from question
{tex}y \in \left[ {\frac{5}{6},\frac{3}{2}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {y - \frac{5}{6}} \right)\left( {y - \frac{3}{2}} \right) \leq 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow (6y - 5)(2y - 3) \leqslant 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 12{y^2} - 18y - 10y + 15 \leqslant 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 12{y^2} - 28y + 15 \leq 0{/tex} .... (ii)
Multiply equation (i) by -1
{tex} \Rightarrow 4{y^2}(c - 1) + 4y(1 - x) + 4c - 1 \leqslant 0{/tex}
Now comparing with (ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 12 = 4(c - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 4

questions.question:
The range of function f (x) = sgn (sin x) + sgn (cos x) + sgn (tan x) + sgn (cot x), $x \neq \frac{n \pi}{2}(n \in \mathrm{I})$ is :
[Note: sgn k denotes signum function of k.]
questions.question_raw:
The range of function f (x) = sgn (sin x) + sgn (cos x) + sgn (tan x) + sgn (cot x), {tex}x \neq \frac{n \pi}{2}(n \in \mathrm{I}){/tex} is :
[Note: sgn k denotes signum function of k.]
questions.explanation:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} {4 ; 0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ {-2 ; \frac{\pi}{2}<x<\pi} \\ {0 ; \pi<x<\frac{3 \pi}{2}} \\ {-2 ; \frac{3 \pi}{2}<x<2 \pi} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Range of f(x) = {-2, 0, 4}

questions.question: If a polynomial function f satisfies the relation log_2 [f(x)] = log_2 $\left(2+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{9}+\ldots . .+\infty\right) \cdot \log _{3}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right)$ and f (10) = 1001, then the value of f (20) is:
questions.question_raw: If a polynomial function f satisfies the relation log2 [f(x)] = log2 {tex}\left(2+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{9}+\ldots . .+\infty\right) \cdot \log _{3}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right){/tex} and f (10) = 1001, then the value of f (20) is:
questions.explanation:
Given relations are
{tex}\log _{2}(f(x))=\log _{2}\left(2+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{9} \ldots \infty\right) \cdot \log _{3}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right){/tex} and f(10) = 1001
when
{tex}\log _{2}(f(x))=\log _{2}\left[2\left(1+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3^{2}} \ldots \infty\right)\right] \log _{3}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log _{2}(f(x))=\log _{2}\left[2 \times\left(\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{3}}\right)\right] \log _{3}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log _{2}(f(x))=\log _{2}\left[2 \times\left(\frac{1}{\frac{2}{3}}\right)\right] \log _{3}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log _{2}(f(x))=\log _{2} 3 \log _{3}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log _{2}(f(x))=\log _{2}\left(1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=1+\frac{f(x)}{f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(10)=1+\frac{f(10)}{f\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)}{/tex}
Now inserting f(10) = 1001
{tex}\Rightarrow 1001=1+\frac{1001}{f\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)=\frac{1001}{1000}=1+\frac{1}{1000}=1+\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)^{3}{/tex}
Inserting {tex}\frac{1}{10}=y{/tex}
f(y) = 1 + y3
When y = 20
f(20) = 1 + 203 = 8001

questions.question: If x = $\frac{4 l}{1+l^{2}}$ and y = $\frac{2-2 l^{2}}{1+l^{2}}$ where l is a parameter and range of f(x, y) = x^2 - xy + y^2 is [a, b] then (a + b) is equal to:
questions.question_raw: If x = {tex}\frac{4 l}{1+l^{2}}{/tex} and y = {tex}\frac{2-2 l^{2}}{1+l^{2}}{/tex} where l is a parameter and range of f(x, y) = x2 - xy + y2 is [a, b] then (a + b) is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Let tan{tex}\theta{/tex} = l
 {tex}As \sin 2\theta = \frac{{2\tan \theta }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}{/tex}
 So, let's make a substitution x = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex}
Now x2 - xy + y2 {tex} = 4{\sin ^2}2\theta + 4{\cos ^2}2\theta - 4\sin 2\theta \cos 2\theta {/tex}
{tex} = 4 - 2\sin 4\theta {/tex}
As we know range({tex}sin\theta{/tex}) = [-1, 1]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Range is 2, 6
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a + b = 8

questions.question: Let a, b are positive real numbers such that a - b = 10, then the smallest value of the constant K for which $\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+a x\right)}-\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+b x\right)}$ < K for all x > 0, is:
questions.question_raw: Let a, b are positive real numbers such that a - b = 10, then the smallest value of the constant K for which {tex}\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+a x\right)}-\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+b x\right)}{/tex} < K for all x > 0, is:
questions.explanation:
Let {tex}f(x)=\sqrt{x^{2}+a x}-\sqrt{x^{2}+b x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x)=\frac{(a-b)}{\sqrt{1+(a / x)}+\sqrt{1+(b / x)}}{/tex} (on rationalising)
{tex}\therefore f(x)_{\max }=\frac{a-b}{2}{/tex}
Hence, K = 5

questions.question:
The sum of all possible solution(s) of the equation $|| x+2|-3|=\operatorname{sgn}\left(1-\left|\frac{(x-2)\left(x^{2}+10 x+24\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x+4)\left(x^{2}+4 x-12\right)}\right|\right)$ is:
[Note: sgn (y) denotes the signum function of y.]
questions.question_raw:
The sum of all possible solution(s) of the equation {tex}|| x+2|-3|=\operatorname{sgn}\left(1-\left|\frac{(x-2)\left(x^{2}+10 x+24\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x+4)\left(x^{2}+4 x-12\right)}\right|\right){/tex} is:
[Note: sgn (y) denotes the signum function of y.]
questions.explanation:
{tex}|| x+2|-3|=\operatorname{sgn}\left(1-\left|\frac{(x-2)(x+6)(x+4)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)(x+4)(x+6)(x-2)}\right|\right){/tex}
{tex}|| x+2|-3|=\operatorname{sgn}\left(1-\left|\frac{1}{x^{2}+1}\right|\right){/tex} {tex}x \neq 2,-4,-6{/tex}
{tex}|| x+2|-3|=1 \Rightarrow|x+2|-3=\pm 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad|x+2|=4,2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x + 2{/tex} = {tex}\pm 4, \pm 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, -4, 0, -6

questions.question:
If the equation (p^2 - 4) (p^2 - 9) x^3 + $\left[\frac{p-2}{2}\right]$ x^2 + (p - 4) (p^2 - 5p + 6)x + ({2p - 1} = 0 is satisfied by all values of x in (0,3] then sum of all possible integral values of ‘p’ is:
[Note: [y] and [y] denote fractional part function and greatest integer function of y respectively.]
questions.question_raw:
If the equation (p2 - 4) (p2 - 9) x3 + {tex}\left[\frac{p-2}{2}\right]{/tex} x2 + (p - 4) (p2 - 5p + 6)x + ({2p - 1} = 0 is satisfied by all values of x in (0,3] then sum of all possible integral values of ‘p’ is:
[Note: [y] and [y] denote fractional part function and greatest integer function of y respectively.]
questions.explanation:
{tex}\left(p^{2}-4\right)\left(p^{2}-9\right)=0 \Rightarrow p=\pm 2, \pm 3{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{p-2}{2}\right]=0 \Rightarrow 2 \leq p<4{/tex}
{tex}|2 p-1|=0 \Rightarrow 2 p-1=n, n \in I{/tex}
{tex}p=\frac{n+1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Possible values of p are 2 and 3.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Required sum = 5

questions.question: Let f(x) = sin^23x - cos^22x and g(x) = 1 + $\frac{1}{2}$ tan^-1 |x|, then the number of values of x in interval [-10$\pi$, 20$\pi$] satisfying the equation f(x) = sgn (g(x)), is:
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = sin23x - cos22x and g(x) = 1 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} tan-1 |x|, then the number of values of x in interval [-10{tex}\pi{/tex}, 20{tex}\pi{/tex}] satisfying the equation f(x) = sgn (g(x)), is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}g(x)=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}|x|+1 \Rightarrow \operatorname{sgn}(g(x))=1{/tex}
{tex}\sin ^{23} x-\cos ^{22} x=1{/tex}
{tex}\sin ^{23} x=1+\cos ^{22} x{/tex} which is possible if sin x = 1 and cos x = 0.
sin x = 1, x {tex}=2 n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}-10 \pi \leq 2 n \pi+\frac{\pi}{2} \leq 20 \pi{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{21}{2} \leq 2 n \leq \frac{39}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{21}{4} \leq n \leq \frac{39}{4} \Rightarrow-5 \leq n \leq 9{/tex}
Hence, number of value of x = 15

questions.question: The maximum value of the function f(x) = $\frac{x^{4}-x^{2}}{x^{6}+2 x^{3}-1}$ where x > 1 is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The maximum value of the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{x^{4}-x^{2}}{x^{6}+2 x^{3}-1}{/tex} where x > 1 is equal to:
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{x^{4}-x^{2}}{x^{6}+2 x^{3}-1}=\frac{x-\frac{1}{x}}{x^{3}+2-\frac{1}{x^{3}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x-1}{\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{3}+1+1+3\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)} \leq \frac{x-\frac{1}{x}}{3\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)+3\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)}=\frac{1}{6}{/tex}

questions.question: If f(x) = $\frac{4 x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{x^{2}+\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2}}, x \geq 0$ then range of f(x) is:

For x > 0, {tex}t \geq 2{/tex}

questions.question:
Let f(x) = $\left[\begin{array}{cc} {x^{2}-3,} & {x \leq-5} \\ {x+\lambda} & {-5<x<-1} \\ {(\mu-7)(|1-x|+|1+x|)} & {-1 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {x+6} & {1<x<5} \\ {3-x^{2}} & {x \geq 5} \end{array}\right.$
If f(x) is an odd function then the value of $(\lambda+\mu)$, is:
questions.question_raw:
Let f(x) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {x^{2}-3,} & {x \leq-5} \\ {x+\lambda} & {-5<x<-1} \\ {(\mu-7)(|1-x|+|1+x|)} & {-1 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {x+6} & {1<x<5} \\ {3-x^{2}} & {x \geq 5} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
If f(x) is an odd function then the value of {tex}(\lambda+\mu){/tex}, is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}f(x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {{x^2} - 3,}&{x \leqslant - 5}&{} \\ {x + \lambda }&{ - 5 < x < - 1}&{} \\ {(\mu - 7)(|1 - x| + |1 + x|)}&{ - 1 \leqslant x \leqslant 1}&{} \\ {x + 6}&{1 < x < 5}&{} \\ {3 - {x^2}}&{x \geqslant 5}&{} \end{array}} \right.{/tex}
{tex}f(-x)=\left[\begin{array}{ll} {x^{2}-3} & {-x \leq-5 \Rightarrow x \geq 5} \\ {-x+\lambda} & {-5<-x<-1 \Rightarrow 1<x<5} \\ {(\mu-7)(|1+x|+|1-x|)} & {-1 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {-x+6} & {1<-x<-1} \\ {3-x^{2}} & {x \leq-5} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}f(x) + f( - x) = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{x \leqslant - 5}&{} \\ {\lambda + 6}&{ - 5 < x < - 1}&{} \\ { - 2(\mu - 7)(|1 - x| + |1 + x|)}&{ - 1 \leqslant x \leqslant 1}&{} \\ {\lambda + 6}&{1 < x < 5}&{} \\ 0&{x \geqslant 5}&{} \end{array}} \right.{/tex}
For f(r) to be an odd function
f(x) + f(-x) = 0 {tex}\forall x \in R{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda =-6{/tex} and {tex}\mu=7 \Rightarrow(\lambda+\mu)=-6+7=1{/tex}

questions.question: If the functions f(x) = (k^2 - 3k + 2) x^2 + (k^2 - 1) $\forall$x$\in$R and g(x) = (k^2 - 6k + 5) x^3 + (k^2 - 2k + 1) x + (k^2 - k) $\forall$x$\in$R have the same graph, then the number of real values of k, is:

questions.explanation:
k2 - Bk + 2 = 0, k2 -1 = 0, k2 - 6k + 5 = 0, lz -zl*1 = 0
and k2 - k = 0 must be satisfied simuitaneously.
So, k = 1
Hence, number of real values of k is one (i.e., k = 1).

questions.question: Let f(x) = max. {sin t : 0 < t < x}, g(x) = min. {sin t : 0 < t < x] and h(x) = [f(x) - g(x)] where [ ] denotes greatest integer function, then the range of h(x) is:
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = max. {sin t : 0 < t < x}, g(x) = min. {sin t : 0 < t < x] and h(x) = [f(x) - g(x)] where [ ] denotes greatest integer function, then the range of h(x) is:
questions.explanation:
f(x) = sinx; {tex}0 \leq x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=1 ; \frac{\pi}{2} \leq x \leq 2 \pi{/tex}
{tex}g(x)=0 ; 0 \leq x \leq \pi{/tex}
{tex}=\sin x ; \pi<x<\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=-1 ; \frac{3 \pi}{2} \leq x \leq 2 \pi{/tex}
h(x) = 0; {tex}0 \leq x<\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=1 ; \frac{\pi}{2} \leq x<\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=2 ; x \geq \frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}
Hence, the range of h(x) is {0, 1, 2}

questions.question: The fundamental period of the function f(x) = 4 cos^4 $\left(\frac{x-\pi}{4 \pi^{2}}\right)$ - 2cos $\left(\frac{x-\pi}{2 \pi^{2}}\right)$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The fundamental period of the function f(x) = 4 cos4 {tex}\left(\frac{x-\pi}{4 \pi^{2}}\right){/tex} - 2cos {tex}\left(\frac{x-\pi}{2 \pi^{2}}\right){/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
Time period of cosx is {tex}2\pi {/tex} & cos2x is {tex}\pi{/tex}

Now cosnx has time period {tex}\pi{/tex} for n = even
cosnx has time period & 2{tex}\pi{/tex} for n = odd
Now cos ax

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Time period of cos ax = {tex}\frac{{2\pi }}{a}{/tex}
which is same for {tex}\cos (ax + b){/tex}
Now coming to question
{tex}{\cos ^4}\left( {\frac{{x - \pi }}{{4{\pi ^2}}}} \right){/tex} has time period {tex}\pi{/tex}
Now {tex}{\cos ^4}\left( {\frac{x}{{4{\pi ^2}}} - \frac{\pi }{{4{\pi ^2}}}} \right){/tex} has time period {tex}\frac{\pi }{a}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{{4{\pi ^2}}}}} = 4{\pi ^3}{/tex}
Now {tex}\cos \left( {\frac{{x - \pi }}{{2{\pi ^2}}}} \right){/tex} has time period
{tex}\frac{{2\pi }}{{\frac{1}{{2{\pi ^2}}}}} = 4{\pi ^3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Time period is same for the both terms
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Time period = {tex}4{\pi ^3}{/tex}

{tex}\log _b^a{/tex} then a > 0

Also {tex}{\log _2}\left( {\frac{{x - 2}}{{3 - x}}} \right) \geq 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 2}}{{3 - x}} \geq 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 2 - 3 \times x}}{{3 - x}} \geq 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{2x - 5}}{{3 - x}} \geq 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x \in \left[ {\frac{5}{2},3} \right){/tex}
Now intersection of two graphs;

{tex}x \in \left[ {\frac{5}{2},3} \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a = \frac{5}{2}{/tex} & b = 3
Hence 2a - b = {tex}2 \times \frac{5}{2} - 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - b = 2

questions.question:
Let R_1 and R_2 be two relations defined as follows:
R_1 = {(a, b) $\in$ R^2 : a^2 + b^2 $\in$ Q) and R_2 = {(a, b) $\in$ R^2 : a^2 + b^2 $\notin$ Q}, where Q is the set of all rational numbers. Then:
questions.question_raw:
Let R1 and R2 be two relations defined as follows:
R1 = {(a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R2 : a2 + b2 {tex}\in{/tex} Q) and R2 = {(a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R2 : a2 + b2 {tex}\notin{/tex} Q}, where Q is the set of all rational numbers. Then:

options.text: R_1 and R_2 are both transitive
Neither R_1 nor R_2 is transitive
R_2 is transitive but R_1 is not transitive
R_1 is transitive but R_2 is not transitive

questions.explanation:
For R1 let a = 1 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}, b = 1 - {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}, c = 81/4
aR1b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 + (1 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})2 + (1 - {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})2 = 6 {tex}\in{/tex} Q
bR1c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + c2 = (1 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})2 + (81/4)2 = 3 {tex}\in{/tex} Q
aR1c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + c2 = (1 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})2 + (81/4)2 = 3 + {tex}4 \sqrt{2} \notin Q{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R1 is not transitive.
For R2 let a = 1 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}, b = {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}, c = 1 - {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
aR2b {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + b2 = (1 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})2 + ({tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})2 = 5 + {tex}2 \sqrt{2} \notin Q{/tex}
bR2c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b2 + c2 = {tex}(\sqrt{2})^{2}+(1-\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex} = 5 - {tex}2 \sqrt{2} \notin Q{/tex}
aR2c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a2 + c2 = (1 + {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex})2 + {tex}(1 -\sqrt{2})^{2}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\in{/tex} Q
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R2 is not transitive.

questions.question: Let N denote the set of all natural numbers. Define two binary relations on N as R_1 = {(x, y) $\in$ N $\times$ N : 2x + y = 10} and R_2 = {(x, y) $\in$ N $\times$ N $\therefore$ x + 2y = 10}. Then,
questions.question_raw: Let N denote the set of all natural numbers. Define two binary relations on N as R1 = {(x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\times{/tex} N : 2x + y = 10} and R2 = {(x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\times{/tex} N {tex}\therefore{/tex} x + 2y = 10}. Then,

options.text: both R_1 and R_2 are symmetric relations
both R_1 and R_2 are transitive relations

questions.explanation:
Here,
R1 = {(x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} N {tex}\times{/tex} A : 2x + y = 10} and
R2 = {(x, y) {tex}\in{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} A : x + 2y = 10}
For R1 ; 2x + y = 10 and x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N
So, possible values for x and y are:
x = 1, y = 8 i.e. (1, 8);
x = 2, y = 6 i.e. (2, 6);
x = 3, y = 4 i.e. (3, 4) and
x = 4, y = 2 i.e. (4, 2).
R1 = {(1, 8), (2, 6), (3, 4), (4, 2)}
Therefore, Range of R1 is {2, 4, 6, 8}
R1 is not symmetric
Also, R1 is not transitive because (3, 4), (4, 2) {tex}\in{/tex} R1 but (3, 2) {tex}\notin{/tex} R1
Thus, options A, B and D are incorrect.
For R2 ; x + 2y = 10 and x, y {tex}\in{/tex} N
So, possible values for x and y are:
x = 8, y = 1 i.e. (8, 1);
x = 6, y = 2 i.e. (6, 2);
x = 4, y = 3 i.e. (4, 3) and
x = 2,y = 4 i.e. (2, 4)
R2 = {(8, 1), (6, 2), (4, 3), (2, 4)}
Therefore, Range of R2 is {1, 2, 3, 4}
R2 is not symmetric and transitive.

questions.question: Let $f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x}{\left(1+x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}$ for n $\ge$ 2 and $g(x)=\underbrace{(\text { fofo...of })}_{f \text { occurs } n \text { times }}(x)$. Then $\int x^{n-2}$g(x)dx equals.
questions.question_raw: Let {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x}{\left(1+x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}{/tex} for n {tex}\ge{/tex} 2 and {tex}g(x)=\underbrace{(\text { fofo...of })}_{f \text { occurs } n \text { times }}(x){/tex}. Then {tex}\int x^{n-2}{/tex}g(x)dx equals.
questions.explanation:
Given: {tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{\left(1+x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}{/tex} for {tex}n \geq 2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f o f(x)=f[f(x)]=f\left[\frac{x}{\left(1+x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{x}{\left(1+x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}}{\left[1+\frac{x^{n}}{1+x^{n}}\right]^{1 / n}}=\frac{x}{\left(1+2 x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}{/tex}
Similarly, f o f o f(x) {tex}=\frac{x}{\left(1+3 x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}{/tex}
Proceeding in the same way, we get
g(x) = f o f o f .... o f(x) {tex}=\frac{x}{\left(1+n x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}}{/tex} (f occurs n times)
Now, {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int x^{n-2} g(x) d x=\int \frac{x^{n-1}}{\left(1+n x^{n}\right)^{1 / n}} d x{/tex}
Let 1 + nxn = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n2xn-1dx = dt
{tex}\therefore I=\frac{1}{n^{2}} \int t^{-1 / n} d t=\frac{1}{n^{2}} \cdot \frac{t^{-\frac{1}{n}+1}}{-\frac{1}{n}+1}+K{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{t^{1-1 / n}}{n-1}+K=\frac{\left(1+n x^{n}\right)^{1-1 / n}}{n(n-1)}+K{/tex}

questions.question: If $F(x)=\left(f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^{2}+\left(g\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^{2}$ where f"(x) = -f(x) and g(x) = f'(x) and given that F(5) = 5, then F(10) is equal to
questions.question_raw: If {tex}F(x)=\left(f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^{2}+\left(g\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^{2}{/tex} where f"(x) = -f(x) and g(x) = f'(x) and given that F(5) = 5, then F(10) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given : {tex}F(x)=\left(f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^{2}+\left(g\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow F^{\prime}(x)=2 f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot f^{\prime}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}+2 g\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot g^{\prime}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot f^{\prime}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+f^{\prime}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} g(x) = f'(x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} g'(x) = f"(x)]
{tex}=f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot f^{\prime}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)-f^{\prime}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right) f\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)=0{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} f"(x) = -f(x)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F(x) is a constant function.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} F(x) = F(5) = 5 {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F(10) = 5

questions.question: Let r be a relation from R (set of real numbers) to R defined by r = {(a, b) : a, b $\in$ R and a - b + $\sqrt{3}$ is an irrational number}. The relation r is
questions.question_raw: Let r be a relation from R (set of real numbers) to R defined by r = {(a, b) : a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R and a - b + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} is an irrational number}. The relation r is
questions.explanation:
r = {(a, b)| a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R and a - b + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} is an irrational number}
Here, r is reflexive as aRa = a - a + {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}
which is an irrational number.
{tex}\sqrt{3} r 1=\sqrt{3}-1+\sqrt{3}=2 \sqrt{3}-1{/tex}, which is an irrational number.
But {tex}\text { 1 } r \sqrt{3}=1-\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{3}=1{/tex} which is not an irrational number.
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{3} r 1 \neq 1 r \sqrt{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} r is not symmetric.
Also, r is not transitive.
Since, {tex}\sqrt{3} r 1{/tex} and {tex}1 r 2 \sqrt{3} \neq \sqrt{3} r 2 \sqrt{3}{/tex}

questions.question: If the function $f: {R} \rightarrow R$ is defined by f(x ) = |x| (x - sin x), then which of the following statements is TRUE?
questions.question_raw: If the function {tex}f: {R} \rightarrow R{/tex} is defined by f(x ) = |x| (x - sin x), then which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: f is both one-one and onto
f is onto, but NOT one-one
f is neither one-one NOR onto
f is one-one, but NOT onto

questions.explanation:
f(x) is a non-periodic, continous and odd fiinction
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} -x^{2}+x \sin x, x<0 \\ x^{2}-x \sin x, x \geq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} -2 x+\sin x+x \cos x, x<0 \\ 2 x-\sin x-x \cos x, x \geq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} -(x-\sin x)-x(1-\cos x), x<0 \\ (x-\sin x)+x(1-\cos x), x>0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} x - sin x < 0 if x < 0 and 1 - cos x > 0, {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -(x - sin x) - x(1 - cos x) > 0 if x < 0 and (x - sin x) + x (1 - cos x) > 0 if x > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) > 0 {tex}\forall{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is increasing in R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is one-one
{tex}\therefore \mathop {\operatorname{Lt} }\limits_{_{x \to - \infty }} \left( { - {x^2}} \right)\left( {1 - \frac{{\sin x}}{x}} \right)=-\infty{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow \infty} x^{2}\left(1-\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)=\infty{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Range of f(x) = R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is an onto function

questions.question: Let f, g and h be real-valued functions defined on the interval [0, 1] by $f(x)=e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}, g(x)=x e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}$ and $h(x)=x^{2} e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}$. If a, b and c denote, respectively, the absolute maximum of f, g and h on [0, 1], then
questions.question_raw: Let f, g and h be real-valued functions defined on the interval [0, 1] by {tex}f(x)=e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}, g(x)=x e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}h(x)=x^{2} e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}{/tex}. If a, b and c denote, respectively, the absolute maximum of f, g and h on [0, 1], then
questions.explanation:
{tex}f(x)=e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=2 x\left(e^{x^{2}}-e^{-x^{2}}\right) \geq 0{/tex}, {tex}\forall \mathbf{x} \in[0,1]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is an increasing function on [0, 1]
{tex}\therefore f_{\max }=f(1)=e+\frac{1}{e}=a ; g(x)=x e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)=\left(2 x^{2}+1\right) e^{x^{2}}-2 x e^{-x^{2}} \geq 0, \forall x \in[0,1]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} g'(x) is an increasing function on [0, 1]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} gmax = g(1) = {tex}e+\frac{1}{e}{/tex} = b
{tex}h(x)=x^{2} e^{x^{2}}+e^{-x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow h^{\prime}(x)=2 x\left[e^{x^{2}}\left(1+x^{2}\right)-e^{-x^{2}}\right] \geq 0, \forall x \in[0,1]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} h(x)  is an increasing funtion on [0, 1]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} hmax = h(1) = {tex}e+\frac{1}{e}{/tex} = c
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = b = c

questions.question: If the functions f(x) and g(x) are defined on R $\rightarrow$ R such that $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & x \in \text { rational } \\ x, & x \in \text { irrational } \end{array}\right.;$ $g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & x \in \text { irrational } \\ x, & x \in \text { rational } \end{array}\right.$ then (f - g) (x) is
questions.question_raw: If the functions f(x) and g(x) are defined on R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R such that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & x \in \text { rational } \\ x, & x \in \text { irrational } \end{array}\right.;{/tex} {tex}g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & x \in \text { irrational } \\ x, & x \in \text { rational } \end{array}\right.{/tex} then (f - g) (x) is
questions.explanation:
Given f(x) and g(x) defined on R {tex}\to{/tex} R
and {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & x \in \text { rational } \\ x, & x \in \text { irrational } \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}g(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 0, & x \in \text { irrational } \\ x, & x \in \text { rational } \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (f - g); R {tex}\to{/tex} R such that
{tex}(f-g)(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -x, & x \in \text { rational } \\ x, & x \in \text { irrational } \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Since (f - g) : R {tex}\to{/tex} R for any x, then there is only one value of (f(x) - g(x)) whether x is rational or irrational.
Moreover as x {tex}\in{/tex} R, f(x) - g(x) also belongs to R. Therefore, (f - g) is one-one onto.

questions.question: Let f(x) = $\frac{x}{1+e^{x}}-\frac{x}{2}$ + 10, then f is
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = {tex}\frac{x}{1+e^{x}}-\frac{x}{2}{/tex} + 10, then f is
questions.explanation:
{tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{1+e^{x}}-\frac{x}{2}+10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(-x)=\frac{-x}{1+e^{-x}}+\frac{x}{2}+10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(-x)=\frac{-x e^{x}}{1+e^{x}}+\frac{x}{2}+10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(-x)=\frac{x-x\left(1+e^{x}\right)}{1+e^{x}}+\frac{x}{2}+10{/tex} {tex}=\frac{x}{1+e^{x}}-x+\frac{x}{2}+10{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{x}{1+e^{x}}-\frac{x}{2}+10{/tex} = f(x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Function f is an even function.

questions.question: The function f defined as f(x) = sin (log (x + $\sqrt{x^{2}+1}$)) is

{tex}\Rightarrow f(-x)=\sin \left[\log \left(-x+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\sin \log \left[\left(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-x\right) \frac{\left(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+x\right)}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+x\right)}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\sin \log \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\sin \left[\log \left(x+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}\right)^{-1}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\sin \left[-\log \left(x+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-\sin \left[\log \left(x+\sqrt{1+x^{2}}\right)\right]{/tex}
= -f(x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Function f is an odd function.

100 < x < 103 and x > 0

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} [102, 103)

{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{1}{y-1}+1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{y}{y-1}}{/tex} or {tex}-\sqrt{\frac{y}{y-1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x is defined when {tex}\frac{y}{y-1} \geq 0{/tex} and y - 1 {tex}\ne{/tex} 0.
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} (y {tex}\le{/tex} 0 or y {tex}\ge{/tex} 1) and y {tex}\ne{/tex} 1
{tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\le{/tex} 0 or y > 1 {tex}\Leftrightarrow y \in(-\infty, 0] \cup(1, \infty){/tex}

Range of f(x) = R - {2}

questions.question: A function f : R $\to$ R satisfies f(x + y) = f(x) . f(y) for all x and y and f(0) $\ne$ 0. A function g is defined as g(x) $=\frac{f(x)}{1+(f(x))^{2}}$, then the function g is

Substituting x = y = 0, we get f(0) = f(0)2

Substituting y = -x, we get f(0) = f(x)f(-x)

Replace x by -x in definition of g,
g(-x) {tex}=\frac{f(-x)}{1+(f(-x))^{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{f(x)}}{1+\frac{1}{(f(x))^{2}}}{/tex}

questions.question: If f : R $\rightarrow$ R is defined by f(x) = $\frac{25^{x}}{5+25^{x}}$, then the value of the expression $f\left(\frac{1}{2019}\right)+f\left(\frac{2}{2019}\right)+\ldots+f\left(\frac{2018}{2019}\right)$ is

f(y) + f(1 - y)
{tex}=\frac{25^{y}}{5+25^{y}}+\frac{25^{1-y}}{5+25^{1-y}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{25^{v}}{5+25^{y}}+\frac{\left(25^{y}\right)\left(25^{1-y}\right)}{5\left(25^{\prime}\right)+25}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{25^{y}}{5+25^{y}}+\frac{5}{25^{y}+5}{/tex}

The required value of the expression

= 1009

and f(2) = 6 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2a + b = 6 ...(ii)
Also, g(-2) = 6 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} -2p + q = 6 ...(iii)
and g(2) = 0 {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} 2p + q = 0 ...(iv)
From (i) and (ii), we get {tex}a=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}, b = 3
From (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}p=-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}, q = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\frac{3}{2} x{/tex} + 3, g(x) = {tex}-\frac{3}{2} x{/tex} + 3
f(x) = g(x) {tex}\Leftrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{2} x{/tex} + 3 = {tex}-\frac{3}{2} x{/tex} + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0

questions.question: If f(x) = x^3 + 5x + 1 for real x, then
questions.question_raw: If f(x) = x3 + 5x + 1 for real x, then

options.text: f is neither one-one nor onto in R
f is one-one but not onto in R
f is one-one and onto in R
f is onto in R but not one-one

questions.explanation:
Let x, y {tex}\in{/tex} R be such that
f(x) = f(y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x3 + 5x + 1 = y3 + 5y - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x3 - y3) + 5(x - y) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) (x2 + xy + y2 + 5) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow (x - y)\left[ {{{\left( {x + \frac{y}{2}} \right)}^2} + \frac{{3{y^2}}}{4} + 5} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y > 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is one-one
Let y be an arbitrary element in R(co-domain).
Then, f(x) = y i.e., x3 + 5x + 1 = y has at least one real root, say {tex}\beta{/tex} in R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\beta^3{/tex} + 5{tex}\beta{/tex} + 1 = y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f({tex}\beta{/tex}) = y
Thus, for each y {tex}\in{/tex} R there exists {tex}\beta \in{/tex} R such that f({tex}\beta{/tex}) = y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is onto
Hence, f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is one-one onto.

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f takes all values from {tex}(-\infty, \infty){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is onto.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f : R {tex}\to{/tex} R is onto but not one-one.

questions.question:
Let k be an odd integer and a function f : Z $\to$ Z be defined as
f(n) = n + 3, n is odd
f(n) = $\frac{n}{2}$, n is even
If f(f(f(k))) = 27, then sum of the digits of k is
questions.question_raw:
Let k be an odd integer and a function f : Z {tex}\to{/tex} Z be defined as
f(n) = n + 3, n is odd
f(n) = {tex}\frac{n}{2}{/tex}, n is even
If f(f(f(k))) = 27, then sum of the digits of k is
questions.explanation:
Given that k is odd.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(k) = k + 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(f(k)) = f(k + 3) ...[{tex}\because{/tex} k + 3 is even]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(f(k))) = {tex}f\left(\frac{k+3}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{k+3}{2}\right){/tex}, if {tex}\frac{k+3}{2}{/tex} is even
{tex}=\frac{k+3}{2}+3{/tex}, if {tex}\frac{k+3}{2}{/tex} is odd
f(f(f(k))) = 27
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k+3}{2}+3=27{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{k+3}{2}\right){/tex} = 27
(if {tex}\frac{k+3}{2}{/tex} is odd) (if {tex}\frac{k+3}{2}{/tex} is even)
{tex}\Rightarrow \underbrace{k=45}_{\left(\text {if } \frac{k+3}{2} \text {is odd}\right)}{/tex} or {tex}\underbrace {k = 105}_{\left( {{\text{if }}\frac{{k + 3}}{2}{\text{ is even}}} \right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 105
(k = 45 rejected as {tex}\frac{45+3}{2}{/tex} = 24, as 24 is an even number)
Sum of the digits of k = 6

Differentiating f with respect to x, we get

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f' changes sign.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is non-monotonic.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is not injective.
(i) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx2 - x + y = 0
Discriminant {tex}\Delta{/tex} = 1 - 4y2 
{tex}x \in \mathrm{R} \Rightarrow \Delta \geq 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Leftrightarrow y^{2} \leq \frac{1}{4} \Leftrightarrow-\frac{1}{2} \leq y \leq \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} The range of f = {tex}\left[-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right]{/tex} = codomain of f
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is surjective

questions.question:
A function f : Z $\to$ R satisfies
f(n + 7) = f(n) for all n $\in$ Z and f(n + 13) = f(n) for all n $\in$ Z then function f
questions.question_raw:
A function f : Z {tex}\to{/tex} R satisfies
f(n + 7) = f(n) for all n {tex}\in{/tex} Z and f(n + 13) = f(n) for all n {tex}\in{/tex} Z then function f
questions.explanation:
f(n) = f(n + 7) for all n {tex}\in{/tex} Z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is periodic and has a period 7.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(n) = f(n + 7) = ... = f(n + 77) ...(i)
Similarly,
f(n) = f(n + 13)= ... = f(n + 78) ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii), we get
f(n + 77) = f(n + 78), for all n.
Replacing n by n - 77, we get
f(n) = f(n + 1) for all n {tex}\in{/tex} Z
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f is a constant function.

= coefficient of x12 in {tex}12 !\left [{ }^{11} C_{0} 1+{ }^{11} C_{1}\left(\frac{x}{2 !}+\frac{x^{2}}{3 !}+\ldots .+\infty\right)\right.{/tex}{tex}\left.+{}^{11}C_2\left(\frac x{2!}+\frac{x^2}{3!}+\dots.+\infty\right)+\dots\right]{/tex}
= coefficient of x12 in {tex}12 !\left[{ }^{11} C_{1}\left(\frac{1}{2 !}\right)\right]=\frac{12 ! \times 11}{2 !}{/tex}

questions.question: If the function f(x) satisfying (f(x))^2 - 4f(x) f(x) + (f'(x))^2 = 0 then f(x) equals
questions.question_raw: If the function f(x) satisfying (f(x))2 - 4f(x) f(x) + (f'(x))2 = 0 then f(x) equals
questions.explanation:
We are given that
(f(x))2 - 4f(x)f'(x) + (f'(x))2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{4 f(x) \pm \sqrt{16(f(x))^{2}-4(f(x))^{2}}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{4 f(x) \pm 2 f(x) \sqrt{4-1}}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{4 f(x) \pm 2 \sqrt{3} f(x)}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=f(x)(2 \pm \sqrt{3}){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{f^{\prime}(x)}{f(x)}=(2 \pm \sqrt{3}){/tex}
Integrating, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log f(x) = {tex}(2 \pm \sqrt{3}) x+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=e^{(2 \pm \sqrt{3}) x+C}{/tex} {tex}=e^{C} e^{(2 \pm \sqrt{3}) x}=\lambda e^{(2 \pm \sqrt{3}) x}{/tex}
where {tex}\lambda{/tex} = eC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lambda e^{(2 \pm \sqrt{3}) x}=\lambda e^{(2+\sqrt{3}) x}{/tex}, {tex}\lambda e^{(2-\sqrt{3}) x}{/tex}

Let the sets A and B denote the domain and range respectively of the function f(x) = $\frac{1}{\sqrt{[x]-x}}$, where [x] denotes the smallest integer greater than or equal to x.
Then among the statements
(S1) : A $\cap$ B = (1, $\infty$) - N and
(S2) : A $\cup$ B = (1, $\infty$)
questions.question_raw:
Let the sets A and B denote the domain and range respectively of the function f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{[x]-x}}{/tex}, where [x] denotes the smallest integer greater than or equal to x.
Then among the statements
(S1) : A {tex}\cap{/tex} B = (1, {tex}\infty{/tex}) - N and
(S2) : A {tex}\cup{/tex} B = (1, {tex}\infty{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Given, f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{[x]-x}}{/tex}
If x {tex}\in{/tex} I, [x] = [x] (greatest integer function)
If x {tex}\notin{/tex} I, [x] = [x] + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow f ( x )=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{\sqrt{[x]-x}}, x \in I \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{[x]+1-x}}, x \notin I \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f ( x )=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{\sqrt{-\{x\}}}, x \in I, \\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\{x\}}}, x \notin I \end{array}\right.{/tex} (does not exit)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} domain of f(x) = R - I = A
Now, f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\{x\}}}, x \notin I{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 < {x} < 1 {tex} \Rightarrow 0<\sqrt{1-\{x\}}<1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\{x\}}}>1{/tex}
So, range = (1, {tex}\infty{/tex}) = B
So, A {tex}\cap{/tex} B = (1, {tex}\infty{/tex}) - N
and, A {tex}\cup{/tex} B {tex}\neq{/tex} (1, {tex}\infty{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S1 is only correct

questions.question: Let f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c be such that f(1) = 3, f(-2) = $\lambda$ and f(3) = 4. If f(0) + f(1) + f(-2) + f(3) = 14, then $\lambda$ is equal to:
questions.question_raw: Let f(x) = ax2 + bx + c be such that f(1) = 3, f(-2) = {tex}\lambda{/tex} and f(3) = 4. If f(0) + f(1) + f(-2) + f(3) = 14, then {tex}\lambda{/tex} is equal to:
questions.explanation:
f(0) + f(1) + f(-2) + f(3) = 14
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(0) + 3 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 4 = 14 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = 7 - {tex}\lambda{/tex} = c
f(1) = a + b + c = 3 ...(i)
f(3) = 9a + 3b + c = 4 ...(ii)
f(-2) = 4a - 2b + c = {tex}\lambda{/tex} ...(iii)
Subtract (iii) from (ii)
a + b = {tex}\frac{4-\lambda}{5}{/tex} put in equation (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{4-\lambda}{5}+7-\lambda=3 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6 \lambda=24 ; {/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 4

questions.question: Let [x] denote the greatest integer $\le$ x, where x $\in$ R. If the domain of the real valued function f(x) = $\sqrt{\frac{|[x]|-2}{|[x]|-3}}$ is ($-\infty$, a) $\cup$ [b, c) $\cup$ [4, $\infty$), a < b < c, then the value of a + b + c is:
questions.question_raw: Let [x] denote the greatest integer {tex}\le{/tex} x, where x {tex}\in{/tex} R. If the domain of the real valued function f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{|[x]|-2}{|[x]|-3}}{/tex} is ({tex}-\infty{/tex}, a) {tex}\cup{/tex} [b, c) {tex}\cup{/tex} [4, {tex}\infty{/tex}), a < b < c, then the value of a + b + c is:
questions.explanation:
To find the domain of the function f(x), {tex}\frac{|[x]|-2}{|[x]|-3} \geq 0{/tex}
Case I: When |[x]| - 2 > 0 and |[x]| - 3 > 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex}(-{tex}\infty{/tex}, -3) {tex}\cup{/tex} [4, {tex}\infty{/tex}) ...(i)
Case II: When |[x]| - 2 {tex}\leq{/tex} 0 and |[x]| - 3 < 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\in{/tex} [-2, -3)
So, from (i) and (ii) domain of function f(x)
= (-{tex}\infty{/tex}, -3) {tex}\cup{/tex} [-2, 3) {tex}\cup{/tex} [4, {tex}\infty{/tex})
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (a + b + c) = -3 + (-2) + 3 = - 2 (a < b < c)

questions.question: Consider a function $f:\mathbb{N}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$, satisfying f(1) + 2f(2) + 3f(3) +...+ xf(x) = x(x + 1) f(x); x $\ge$ 2 with f(1) = 1. Then $\frac{1}{ f (2022)}+\frac{1}{ f (2028)}$ is equal to

Given for x {tex}\ge{/tex} 2
f(1) + 2f(2) + 3f(3) +...+ xf(x) = x(x + 1) f(x) ...(i)
replace x by x + 1, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(x + 1) f(x) + (x + 1) f(x + 1)
= (x + 1) (x + 2) f(x + 1)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{f(x+1)}+\frac{1}{f(x)}=\frac{(x+2)}{f(x)}{/tex}
Put x = 2 in eqn. (i), f(1) + 2f(2) = 2(2 + 1) f(2)
1 + 2f(2) = 6f(2) f(2) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
f(2) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}, f(3) = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
Now f(2022) = {tex}\frac{1}{4044}{/tex}; f(2028) = {tex}\frac{1}{4056}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{1}{f(2022)}+\frac{1}{f(2028)}{/tex} = 4044 + 4056 = 8100

questions.question: If set A = {1, 2} and set B = {a, b}, then cartesian product of set and A set B is given by
questions.question_raw: If set A = {1, 2} and set B = {a, b}, then cartesian product of set and A set B is given by
questions.explanation:
The set of all ordered pairs (a, b) such that {tex}a \in A{/tex} and {tex}b \in B{/tex} is called cartesian product of sets A and B.

questions.question: If the relation R in the set {1, 2, 3, 4} given by R = {(1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 4), (3, 1), (3, 2), (4, 3), (4, 2)} then domain of R is given by
